text_blocks.text: Many early immigrant groups traveled to
America to worship freely, particularly after the
English Civil War and religious conflict in France
and Germany. They included nonconformists like the
Puritans, who were Protestant Christians fleeing
religious persecution from the Anglican King of
England. Despite a common background, the groups'
views on religious toleration were mixed. While some
such as Roger Williams of Rhode Island and William
Penn of Pennsylvania ensured the protection of
religious minorities within their colonies, others like
the Plymouth Colony and Massachusetts Bay Colony
had established churches. The Dutch colony of New
Netherland established the Dutch Reformed Church
and outlawed all other worship, though enforcement
was sparse. Religious conformity was desired partly
for financial reasons: the established Church was
responsible for poverty relief, putting dissenting
